title: Spanakopita
subtitle: Spinach and feta filo pie
excerpt: Spanakopita is a Greek filo pastry pie filled with wilted spinach, crumbled feta, eggs, and herbs. The pastry bakes up crisp and golden with a soft, salty filling inside. It cuts into portions well and is as good cold as it is warm.
sourceNotes: A staple of Greek home cooking, appearing in variations across every region. Filo pastry pies with greens and cheese are part of a broader tradition of Greek pites. The recipe here follows the baked-tray format common in home kitchens.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unbaked assembled pie, well-wrapped. Bake from frozen at 180°C for about 1 hour. Or freeze baked portions once cooled.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 8 hours ahead and refrigerate unbaked.

glossaryTerms.definition: Very thin sheets of unleavened pastry dough used in Greek, Turkish, and Middle Eastern cooking. Layers are brushed with butter or oil to create a crisp, flaky result when baked.

content.text: Spanakopita is a Greek pie of crisp, buttered

layered with wilted spinach and crumbled feta. It bakes into a rich, salty filling inside a shatteringly crisp casing. The main thing to keep in mind is working quickly with the filo so the sheets don't dry out and crack.
The filling needs the moisture squeezed out thoroughly. Wet spinach turns the pastry soggy. Get that right and the rest is straightforward.
A rectangular baking tin about 30 cm × 22 cm gives a good depth. Keep a damp tea towel over the unused filo sheets as you work so they don't dry out.

Wilt the spinach in a large pan over high heat in batches, using only the water clinging to the leaves. As each batch wilts, transfer to a colander. Press firmly with the back of a spoon to squeeze out as much liquid as possible. Once cool enough to handle, squeeze in your fists to remove more moisture. Roughly chop and set aside.

Combine the wilted spinach, spring onions, crumbled feta, beaten eggs, parsley, dill, {{nutmeg}} of grated nutmeg, and black pepper in a large bowl. Mix well. Taste for seasoning; the feta is salty so you may not need extra salt.
Heat the oven to 190°C. Mix the melted butter with the olive oil in a small bowl. Brush the bottom and sides of the baking tin with this mixture.

Lay one sheet of filo in the tin, letting it drape over the sides. Brush lightly with the butter mixture. Repeat with 5 more sheets, brushing each one. If a sheet tears, patch it with a small piece from another sheet and brush over it.
Spread the spinach filling evenly over the filo base. Fold the overhanging filo back over the filling.

Lay the remaining 6 filo sheets on top, brushing each one with the butter mixture. Tuck the edges down the sides of the tin. Score the top layer of filo into portions with a sharp knife before baking; this makes it much easier to cut once cooked.
Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until deep golden and crisp. Rest for 10 minutes before cutting along the scored lines.

items.cause: Filling too wet, or butter not applied to the base sheets
items.fix: The single most important step is squeezing the spinach as dry as possible. Press it firmly, then squeeze again. Brush the base sheets generously with butter before adding the filling.

items.symptom: The filo tears when I handle it
items.fix: Keep unused sheets covered with a lightly damp tea towel. Work quickly once you have a sheet out in the air.
Taste the raw filling and adjust. Feta varies in saltiness; less-salty feta needs a pinch of salt in the filling. The herbs make a real difference; use both parsley and dill.

items.symptom: It's impossible to cut once baked
items.fix: Score the top layers of filo before the pie goes in the oven. If you forgot, use a very sharp knife and cut with a gentle sawing motion once the pie is completely cool.

content.text: Spanakopita is one of a family of Greek filo pies called pites, each named for its filling. It appears at family tables, at street-food counters in small triangular parcels, and on restaurant mezze spreads. Cold portions pack well for a picnic or lunchbox. It is one of the dishes that people who have visited Greece most often try to recreate at home.
